back: ~30 multiple-choice questions PLUS 3 hands-on lab exercises, 120 minutes total, $500, valid 2 years
Installation & Deployment 31%; Administration 23%; Workload Management 23%; Troubleshooting & Optimization 23%

front: BCM: what runs on the head node?
back: CMDaemon (management), provisioning services (PXE/TFTP/DHCP), monitoring, software image store; optionally HA pair via cmha

front: BCM: node category vs software image
back: Image = the OS filesystem provisioned to nodes; category = group of nodes sharing settings incl. which image, disk layout, network config
PXE boot from provisioning node -> node-installer environment -> identify node/category -> partition disk -> sync software image -> boot into installed OS -> report to CMDaemon
cmsh (shell) and Base View (GUI); both talk to CMDaemon on the head node
cmsh -c "device; list"

front: cmsh: make a config change persist
back: Run commit after set/add; uncommitted changes are staged only
device, category, softwareimage, network, user, partition, monitoring, main
device mode: power -n node003 on (via the node's BMC/IPMI)

front: BCM: correct way to patch packages on all compute nodes
back: Chroot into the software image (cm-chroot-sw-img), update packages there, then imageupdate or reboot nodes — never hand-patch live nodes

front: BCM: preview what imageupdate would change
back: imageupdate --dry-run (shows the rsync delta between image and node)
cm-wlm-setup
cm-kubernetes-setup

front: BCM: where are BCM users stored?
back: BCM-managed LDAP, administered via cmsh user mode (add/set/commit) or Base View

front: BCM: node stuck in node-installer — first checks
back: DHCP/PXE/TFTP reachability on the provisioning network; node identification/category and image assignment; CMDaemon log /var/log/cmdaemon and BMC console
No free tier; product license per node — study via Administrator Manual docs, drill equivalents on plain Slurm/K8s

front: Slurm: three core daemons and location
back: slurmctld (controller node), slurmd (every compute node), slurmdbd (accounting DB front-end, fronts MySQL/MariaDB)
Authentication service; identical /etc/munge/munge.key on all nodes and synced clocks are required or RPCs fail

front: Slurm: config line enabling GPU tracking cluster-wide
back: GresTypes=gpu in slurm.conf plus Gres=gpu:<type>:<count> on NodeName lines
select/cons_tres (trackable resources incl. GPUs, per-CPU/memory allocation)

front: gres.conf: two ways to define GPUs
back: AutoDetect=nvml (NVML discovery) or explicit: Name=gpu Type=a100 File=/dev/nvidia0 [Cores=...]

front: Slurm: minimal node + partition lines for 1-GPU node
back: NodeName=node01 CPUs=8 RealMemory=30000 Gres=gpu:l4:1 State=UNKNOWN / PartitionName=gpu Nodes=node01 Default=YES State=UP
slurmdbd -> slurmctld -> slurmd; newer daemons accept older RPC (two versions back), not vice versa
Install containerd -> kubeadm init --pod-network-cidr -> install CNI plugin -> kubeadm join workers

front: kubeadm: pods Pending / node NotReady right after init
back: No CNI installed — kubeadm never installs one; apply Calico/Flannel/Cilium matching the pod CIDR

front: GPU Operator: component stack in dependency order
back: NFD/GFD labels -> driver DaemonSet -> container toolkit -> device plugin -> DCGM exporter (+ MIG Manager, validators)
helm repo add nvidia https://helm.ngc.nvidia.com/nvidia && helm install gpu-operator nvidia/gpu-operator -n gpu-operator --create-namespace

front: GPU Operator: when to set driver.enabled=false
back: Host already has the NVIDIA driver (DGX OS, cloud GPU images, admin-managed) — operator then skips the driver DaemonSet

front: GPU Operator: what does the device plugin do?
back: Advertises nvidia.com/gpu (or MIG resources) to kubelet so pods can request GPUs via resources.limits

front: GPU Operator: what does the container toolkit component do?
back: Installs nvidia-container-runtime on the node and rewrites containerd config so containers can be injected with GPU devices
Container Device Interface — vendor-neutral JSON specs describing device nodes/mounts/hooks; nvidia-ctk generates them; modern replacement for runtime hook injection

front: GPU Operator: single CRD that configures everything
back: ClusterPolicy (driver version, toolkit, device plugin config, mig.strategy, dcgm exporter...)
9400, /metrics (Prometheus format)

front: DCGM metric names: GPU util / FB memory used / Xid count
back: DCGM_FI_DEV_GPU_UTIL, DCGM_FI_DEV_FB_USED, DCGM_FI_DEV_XID_ERRORS

front: Four networks in a GPU cluster
back: Management/provisioning (PXE, BCM, SSH); out-of-band BMC/IPMI; high-speed compute fabric (IB/RoCE, NCCL); storage network
Each of the 8 NICs per node connects to its own leaf plane so GPU i talks to GPU i one hop away — matches NCCL ring/tree traffic, avoids congestion

front: K8s: driver upgrade strategy with GPU Operator
back: Bump driver version in ClusterPolicy; operator rolls node by node (drain/reload) — no manual per-node installs

front: Slurm: give user alice access under account research
back: sacctmgr add account research; sacctmgr add user alice account=research (association required when enforcement is on)

front: NVIDIA official prep resources for NCP-AIO
back: DLI 'AI Infrastructure and Operations' courses, BCM Administrator Manual, GPU Operator docs, Run:ai docs, Slurm/DCGM docs

front: Slurm: drain a node with a reason / return to service
back: scontrol update nodename=gpu01 state=drain reason="maint" ; scontrol update nodename=gpu01 state=resume

front: sinfo states: idle / mix / alloc / drain
back: idle=free, mix=partially allocated, alloc=fully allocated, drain(ing)=no new work (drng while jobs finish)

front: Slurm: create QOS with 30min wall limit and 2-GPU-per-user cap
back: sacctmgr add qos short MaxWall=00:30:00 MaxTRESPerUser=gres/gpu=2; attach: sacctmgr modify account X set qos+=short
(cluster, account, user[, partition]) record in slurmdbd carrying limits, fairshare and QOS; without one, submissions can be rejected
priority/multifactor weighs recent decayed usage vs allocated share — heavy users sink, underserved accounts rise
sacct = per-job accounting records; sreport = aggregated usage reports (by cluster/account/user over time)
PreemptType=preempt/qos with QOS Preempt settings — higher QOS can requeue/cancel lower QOS jobs
Unit of quota assignment mapped to a Kubernetes namespace; workloads submit into a project; quotas set per project
Grouping level above projects; quotas can be set at department level and shared among its projects
Projects can consume idle GPUs beyond their quota; that usage is preemptible and reclaimed when the owning project needs it
Training/over-quota workloads are preemptible (evict + requeue); non-preemptible (e.g. inference/interactive within quota) cannot exceed quota
Multiple workloads share one GPU with per-workload GPU memory limits enforced in software — no hardware isolation (contrast MIG)
KAI is the open-sourced scheduling core of Run:ai: queues with quota/over-quota weights, PodGroup gang scheduling, preemption/reclaim

front: KAI: how a pod joins a queue
back: Label kai.scheduler/queue: <queue-name> plus schedulerName: kai-scheduler; podgrouper auto-creates the PodGroup
All pods of a workload are placed atomically or none are — prevents deadlocked partial allocations of distributed jobs
Role is namespaced; ClusterRole is cluster-wide and needed for cluster-scoped resources (e.g. nodes)

front: K8s: limit namespace team-a to 4 GPUs
back: ResourceQuota with spec.hard: requests.nvidia.com/gpu: "4" in that namespace
cordon = mark unschedulable only; drain = cordon + evict pods (respects PodDisruptionBudgets, needs --ignore-daemonsets)
GPU Instance = hardware partition of memory+SM slices (fault isolated); Compute Instance = subdivision of a GI's SMs sharing its memory
1g.5gb x7, 2g.10gb x3, 3g.20gb x2, 4g.20gb x1, 7g.40gb x1 (80GB card doubles memory per profile)
nvidia-smi -i 0 -mig 1 (reset needed) then nvidia-smi mig -cgi 3g.20gb,3g.20gb -C; verify nvidia-smi mig -lgi

front: nvidia-smi mig: list profiles / destroy everything
back: -lgip lists profiles; teardown: nvidia-smi mig -dci then -dgi (CIs before GIs)
kubectl label node <n> nvidia.com/mig.config=all-3g.20gb --overwrite; MIG Manager drives nvidia-mig-parted per its ConfigMap
single: uniform profiles advertised as nvidia.com/gpu; mixed: per-profile resources like nvidia.com/mig-3g.20gb
A30, A100, H100/H200, B200/GB-class — NOT T4, A10, L4, L40S (no MIG hardware on those)

front: MIG vs time-slicing vs MPS (one line each)
back: MIG: hardware partitions, full isolation; time-slicing: alternating full-GPU contexts, no isolation; MPS: concurrent processes share SMs, memory limits but no fault isolation
8 GPUs fully connected via NVLink through NVSwitch, 8 IB/RoCE rail NICs, NVMe scratch, dual CPUs; Fabric Manager supervises the NVSwitch fabric
Standardized building block (32 nodes + leaf switches + mgmt) replicated and joined by a spine to scale predictably
Registry nvcr.io, username literally $oauthtoken, password = NGC API key

front: NGC: K8s pull secret for nvcr.io
back: kubectl create secret docker-registry ngc-secret --docker-server=nvcr.io --docker-username='$oauthtoken' --docker-password=$NGC_API_KEY + imagePullSecrets
Pinned, co-tested CUDA+cuDNN+NCCL+framework per monthly tag (e.g. 25.06-py3); only external requirement is a new-enough host driver

front: NGC CLI: configure and list images
back: ngc config set (API key/org); ngc registry image list nvidia/*; ngc registry model download-version ...
nvcr.io/<org>/[<team>/]<image> — org/team scoping with API-key-based access

front: sbatch: 2 nodes, 4 GPUs per node, 4 tasks per node
back: #SBATCH --nodes=2 --ntasks-per-node=4 --gres=gpu:4 (or --gpus-per-node=4); launch ranks with srun
--gres is per node; --gpus is a job-wide total that may span nodes

front: How Slurm confines a job to its GPUs
back: Sets CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES per step and (with cgroup ConstrainDevices) blocks other GPU device files
NVIDIA's rootless container runtime for HPC + the Slurm SPANK plugin: srun --container-image=nvcr.io#nvidia/pytorch:25.06-py3

front: K8s: request 1 GPU in a pod
back: resources.limits: nvidia.com/gpu: 1 (requests=limits for GPUs; no fractions via device plugin)
TrainJob (user intent: model, ranks) + TrainingRuntime (admin template: image, launcher, gang policy) — v2 of the training operator
<repo>/<model>/config.pbtxt + <repo>/<model>/<version>/model.<fmt>; server flag --model-repository
8000 HTTP, 8001 gRPC, 8002 metrics; readiness GET /v2/health/ready
docker run --gpus all -e NGC_API_KEY -p 8000:8000 nvcr.io/nim/<org>/<model>:tag — OpenAI-compatible /v1/chat/completions

front: NIM: how it picks an engine
back: Detects GPU and selects a pre-optimized profile (TensorRT-LLM or vLLM backend) for that hardware automatically
Device plugin ConfigMap: sharing.timeSlicing.resources[name=nvidia.com/gpu].replicas=N; node then advertises N x the physical GPUs
Device plugin = opaque count (nvidia.com/gpu: 2); DRA (GA K8s 1.34) = DeviceClass + ResourceClaim with CEL selectors on attributes (model, memory, MIG profile)

front: Whole GPU vs MIG vs time-slice vs MPS vs fractions: pick for hard multi-tenant isolation
back: MIG — only option with hardware memory/fault isolation between tenants

front: Interactive dev cluster, many idle notebooks on scarce GPUs: sharing choice
back: Time-slicing or Run:ai fractions — utilization over isolation; MIG wastes fixed slices, whole GPUs waste idle time

front: Docker: 'could not select device driver with capabilities [[gpu]]'
back: Docker has no GPU runtime: install nvidia-container-toolkit, nvidia-ctk runtime configure --runtime=docker, restart docker
sudo nvidia-ctk runtime configure --runtime=containerd && systemctl restart containerd (adds nvidia runtime to config.toml)

front: Pod schedules but sees no GPU devices inside
back: Runtime not applied: missing runtimeClassName: nvidia (when nvidia isn't default runtime) or toolkit misconfigured on that node

front: 'Failed to initialize NVML: Driver/library version mismatch'
back: Userspace driver libs updated while old kernel module still loaded — reboot (or rmmod/modprobe nvidia stack)

front: 'CUDA driver version is insufficient for CUDA runtime version'
back: Container/app CUDA is newer than host driver supports — upgrade driver or use older CUDA image

front: K8s: 'Insufficient nvidia.com/gpu' but GPU is idle — 3 causes
back: Device plugin down (capacity 0); GPU requested by another pod (K8s counts requests not util); resource-name mismatch (MIG mixed / time-slicing not applied)
nvidia-fabricmanager service programs NVLink/NVSwitch routing on HGX/DGX baseboards; required before CUDA init on NVSwitch systems
nvidia-smi looks fine but CUDA apps fail init ('system not yet initialized') on 8-GPU NVSwitch box; check systemctl status nvidia-fabricmanager
FM package version must exactly match the driver version; mismatch = FM won't start (see /var/log/fabricmanager.log)

front: When is Fabric Manager NOT needed?
back: No NVSwitch: single-GPU, PCIe-only multi-GPU, or direct NVLink without switch
GPU utilization sawtooths (0-100%) while dataloaders starve; step time dominated by input wait

front: fio: sequential read benchmark that bypasses cache
back: fio --name=seq --rw=read --bs=1M --size=10G --numjobs=4 --iodepth=32 --direct=1 (O_DIRECT avoids page-cache lies)

front: Fixes for slow training input pipeline
back: Stage dataset to local NVMe, more dataloader workers/prefetch, large sequential reads, sharded formats (WebDataset/TFRecord), parallel FS instead of NFS
DMA path from NVMe/storage directly to GPU memory bypassing CPU bounce buffers — cuFile API; helps IO-bound pipelines
NCCL_DEBUG=INFO (or WARN), NCCL_DEBUG_SUBSYS=INIT,NET,GRAPH for targeted subsystems
Intra-node: NVLink/P2P -> SHM; inter-node: NET/IB (RDMA) -> NET/Socket (TCP). 'via ...' lines in NCCL_DEBUG show the choice
Wrong/asymmetric interface (or docker0 match) makes bootstrap advertise unreachable IPs -> hang at init; set real iface or exclude: ^docker,lo
Intra-node GPU traffic falls back to SHM via host memory — bus bandwidth collapses; visible as 'via SHM' instead of 'via P2P'
all_reduce_perf -b 8 -e 1G -f 2 -g <ngpus>; judge busbw against hardware expectation (NVLink hundreds of GB/s, PCIe ~tens)
-r 1 seconds (software sanity), -r 2 ~2-3min (+PCIe+brief stress), -r 3 30+min (full memory/stress, pre-RMA), -r 4 extended
dcgmi discovery -l (GPUs DCGM sees); dcgmi health --set a; dcgmi health --check; dcgmi dmon for live metrics
Application-side: graphics engine exception / GPU memory page fault (bad pointer) / app abort — fix the app, GPU usually healthy
Double-bit ECC error — hardware memory fault: drain node, dcgmi diag -r 3, RMA path if recurring
Row remapping: 63 = row remapped, pending reset (reboot -> healthy); 64 = remap failed/no spare rows -> drain + RMA
NVLink error — check link state, NVSwitch/Fabric Manager, cabling/baseboard; hardware path
GPU fallen off the bus — PCIe/power/thermal event; check dmesg + BMC sensors, reseat/replace; hardware
Contained / uncontained ECC error: 94 = error contained to one app (others fine); 95 = uncontained, GPU needs reset/drain
Kernel log (dmesg: 'NVRM: Xid ...'), nvidia-smi, DCGM metric DCGM_FI_DEV_XID_ERRORS
dmesg -> classify Xid app-vs-HW -> drain node (kubectl drain / scontrol drain) -> dcgmi diag -r 3 -> pass: resume, fail: RMA

front: Slow H2D transfers on one node — check what?
back: PCIe link downtraining: nvidia-smi -q link width/gen (x16/Gen4 expected) or lspci -vv; riser/slot/BIOS issues
3 labs x 25 min = 75 min; ~30 MCQ in ~45 min (90 s each); never sink 5 min into one MCQ; verify every lab step before moving on
